‚Ä≤ = 200. During training, we generate at 40 milestones a set of 10,000 samples to assess
generalization and memorization. Examples of samples obtained from a model trained on n = 1024
15
samples with base width W = 32 are shown in the middle and right blocks from Fig. 6 for two
training times, ŌĄ = 190K and ŌĄ = 1.62M. At ŌĄ = 190K the model generalizes (fmem = 0%) and
achieve a test FID of 35.1. After too much training, memorization sets in and, by ŌĄ = 1.62M steps,
nearly half the generated samples reproduce training images (fmem = 47.2%).
Statistical evaluation. FIDs [19] are computed‚Ä† using 10,000 generated samples and 10,000 test
samples, averaged over 5 independent runs with disjoint test sets. Error bars in the MT denote twice
the standard deviation. Training and test losses are estimated similarly over 5 repeated evaluation
on n training samples and 2048 test samples, and give negligible confidence intervals. For the
memorization fraction fmem(ŌĄ ), we report the standard error on the mean obtained via bootstrap
resampling of the 10,000 generated samples. We also verified that the scaling in the memorization
time ŌĄmem is insensitive to the choice of the threshold k used to define fmem in Eq. 6 by testing larger
and lower values.
Computing resources. Most trainings were performed on Nvidia H100 GPUs (80GB of memory).
A typical run of 2M steps takes approximately 50 hours on two GPUs and vary with the model
size (defined through its base width W). In total, we train 18 distinct models for the several n, W
configurations of the MT. The longest training (n = 32768 and W = 32 in Fig. 2) ran for 11M steps.
The generation of 10, 000 samples over 40 training times takes around an additional hour per model
on the same hardware support.
A.2 Batch-size effect: repetition vs. memorization
All the experiments in the MT use a fixed batch size B = 512, and in Sect 2 we emphasize that
the observed O(n) scaling of ŌĄmem cannot be explained by repetition over training samples. To
validate this statement, the left panel of Fig. 7 shows FID and memorization fraction curves when we
train the models with full-batch updates (B = n) for n ‚ąą [128, 512]. At any fixed ŌĄ , every sample
has been seen exactly ŌĄ times. Yet ŌĄmem continues to grow linearly with n, as shown in the inset.
This demonstrates that larger datasets reshape the loss landscape ‚Äď requiring proportionally more
updates to overfit ‚Äď rather than simply increasing memorization through repeated exposure of training
samples.
A.3 What about Adam?
We conclude this section by repeating our analysis at fixed W = 64 using the Adam optimizer [28]
instead of SGD with momentum. The learning rate is ő∑ = 1 √ó 10‚ąí4
, gradient averages take values
(ő≤1, ő≤2) = (0.9, 0.999), and batch size B = min(512, n). We keep all other settings and evaluation
metrics as above. As shown in the right panel of Fig. 7, Adam yields the same two-phase training
dynamics with first a generalization regime with fmem = 0 and good performances (small FID), and
later a memorization phase at ŌĄmem ‚ąĚ n, as shown in the inset. The only difference is that both ŌĄgen
and ŌĄmem occur after much fewer steps compared to SGD. This also points out that the emergence of
the two well-separated timescales and their scaling is a fundamental property of the loss landscape.

and alternative score model that U-Net architectures.

B.2 Scaling of ŌĄmem and ŌĄgen with n and W
In Fig. 9, the left panel shows how the KL divergence and memorization fraction evolve with training
time ŌĄ for different training set sizes n at fixed width W = 128, while the right panel fixes n = 2048
and varies W. In both cases, we observe two distinct phases. First, the KL divergence decreases to
near zero on a timescale ŌĄgen independent of n during which the model fully generalizes (fmem = 0).
Beyond ŌĄgen, both DKL(Pőł|P0) and fmem begin to rise at a time ŌĄmem that scales linearly with n, as
highlighted by the inset of the left panel. In contrast, ŌĄmem scales with W‚ąí1
, as shown in the inset of
the right panel. While the precise dependence of ŌĄgen with W remains inconclusive in this setting
and require a more careful analysis, these results on the GMM mirror the main findings of the MT:
the training dynamics of DMs unfolds first in a generalization phase and only later ‚Äď at ŌĄmem ‚ąĚ n/W
‚Äď memorization begins.
B.3 Discussion on conditional diffusion models
Conditional generation aims to sample from distributions of the form p(x|y), where y denotes a
conditioning variable such as a class label, a text embedding, or any other contextual information.
DMs can naturally be extended to this setting using for instance classifier-free guidance [21]. Although conditioning often improves sample quality in practice, memorization effects have also been
observed in models trained conditionally [51, 61, 10]. Our analysis does not rely on the model being
unconditional since these variables typically enter the model as additional inputs and we expect
our result to hold in this setting as well. To investigate it, we train a classifier-free guidance model
to generate sample from our Gaussian mixture conditionally on the class label, and compute the
memorized fraction as a function of ŌĄ that we report in Fig. 10. In the inset, when rescaling the
training time by n, the curves for n ‚ąą {256, 512, 1024} all collapse perfectly, confirming that the
phenomenon persists in the conditional setting. For more complex datasets, ŌĄmem and ŌĄgen may in
fact depend on the conditioning variable and intermediate regimes could exist where certain classes
have already entered the generalization (or memorization) phase while others have not yet.

C Proofs of the analytical results
In the following we provide the mathematical arguments and the proofs for the statement in the
MT. The section using the replica method is not mathematically rigorous but uses a well established
method of theoretical physics, which has been already shown to provide correct results in several
cases. The final result is rigorous, since it can alternatively be obtained from the rigorous free random
matrix approach of [15], as shown in Sect. C.4.

distribution and noising each one N times. The error on the score was evaluated by drawing 10,000
points from the noisy distribution Pt = N (0, őď

C‚Äôest exactement la structure de la deterministic annealing de Rose, Gurewitz et Fox : on minimise une √©nergie/distorsion plus un terme entropique, et en refroidissant on observe des transitions de phase o√Ļ les prototypes se s√©parent. 4. O√Ļ √ßa a d√©j√† √©t√© fait en physique statistique ?
Il y a quatre traditions tr√®s proches.

Premi√®re tradition : deterministic annealing / rate‚Äďdistortion / clustering.
C‚Äôest probablement la r√©f√©rence la plus proche de ta ‚Äútemp√©rature parfaite‚ÄĚ. On consid√®re une free energy

F
T
=
D
‚ąí

,

‚Äč
 =D‚ąíTH,
o√Ļ 
D
D est une distorsion et 
H
H une entropie d‚Äôaffectation. En refroidissant, les clusters se divisent par transitions de phase. C‚Äôest exactement analogue √† ‚Äúles experts doivent d‚Äôabord √™tre identiques, puis se sp√©cialiser au bon moment‚ÄĚ. 

Deuxi√®me tradition : soft committee machines et specialization transitions.
Les vieux papiers de physique statistique sur les r√©seaux √† plusieurs unit√©s cach√©es montrent des transitions ‚Äúunspecialized ‚Üí specialized‚ÄĚ. Pour les soft committee machines, l‚Äôanalyse replica montre une transition de sp√©cialisation ; pour 
K
=
2
K=2, elle est de second ordre, tandis que pour 
K
>
2
K>2, elle peut devenir de premier ordre et m√©tastable. 

Troisi√®me tradition : statistical mechanics of mixture of experts.
Kang et Oh ont √©tudi√© les MoE par physique statistique : en dessous d‚Äôun nombre critique d‚Äôexemples, les experts restent sym√©triques/non sp√©cialis√©s ; au-dessus, la gating network brise la sym√©trie et partitionne l‚Äôespace d‚Äôentr√©e. Ils observent m√™me plusieurs transitions dans les MoE hi√©rarchiques. 

Quatri√®me tradition : REM / verre / condensation.
C‚Äôest la partie Biroli‚ÄďM√©zard : l‚Äôempirical score exact induit un paysage o√Ļ la somme sur les datapoints devient un Random Energy Model. Le collapse est une condensation de type verre, o√Ļ la masse est domin√©e par quelques √©tats/datapoints. 

Ce qui manque, √† ma connaissance, c‚Äôest la synth√®se exacte :

deterministic annealing WTA
+
score-based diffusion
+
speciation/collapse

6. Le schedule optimal devrait probablement √™tre non monotone en temps de diffusion
C‚Äôest l√† que ton intuition peut devenir forte.

Au d√©but du backward process, grand bruit : les classes ne sont pas encore identifiables. Il faut une temp√©rature haute, sinon les experts se s√©parent arbitrairement.

8. Le lien avec la ‚ÄúNishimori line‚ÄĚ
Un mot-cl√© tr√®s important √† regarder est Nishimori line.

Dans les probl√®mes teacher‚Äďstudent bay√©siens, la temp√©rature optimale est souvent celle qui correspond au vrai bruit du mod√®le g√©n√©ratif. Quand le student utilise la bonne temp√©rature/posterior, on est sur la ligne de Nishimori ; hors de cette ligne, on peut cr√©er des phases vitreuses ou des comportements sous-optimaux. La litt√©rature moderne d‚Äôinf√©rence statistique utilise ce langage pour parler de Bayes-optimalit√©, de replica symmetry, et de transitions algorithmiques.

9. Ce qui est vraiment nouveau dans ton id√©e
Je formulerais la contribution comme √ßa :

We reinterpret annealed MCL for score-based diffusion as deterministic annealing in the space of denoising score fields. In high-dimensional Gaussian mixtures, we derive two critical inverse temperatures: a specialization threshold where experts break symmetry and align with mixture components, and a glass threshold where WTA assignments condense on empirical samples. Combining this with finite-time score-learning dynamics yields a diffusion-time- and training-time-dependent safe annealing corridor. This explains why hard WTA and resilient MCL often produce strong but degenerate single-expert solutions, while annealed WTA can realize a genuine mixture only when its temperature schedule stays inside this corridor.

√áa, c‚Äôest beaucoup plus fort que ‚Äúon a test√© plusieurs routing strategies‚ÄĚ.

_**Agent (default)**_

C‚Äôest en place et ex√©cut√© de bout en bout ‚úÖ

J‚Äôai impl√©ment√© et lanc√© automatiquement les calculs **Step B** (\(\beta_{\mathrm{spec}}(t,\tau)\)) et **Step C** (\(\beta_{\mathrm{glass}}(t,\tau)\)), avec sauvegarde compl√®te des quantit√©s + plots.

### Fichiers cr√©√©s

- `tools/rf_theory_core.py`  
  - cŇďur des calculs (grilles, scalaires GMM, Monte Carlo RF, \(\beta_{\mathrm{spec}}\), \(\beta_{\mathrm{glass}}\), formule approx + exacte)
- `tools/rf_step_b_beta_spec.py`  
  - calcule/sauvegarde Step B
- `tools/rf_step_c_beta_glass.py`  
  - calcule/sauvegarde Step C
- `tools/rf_run_all_theory.py`  
  - runner global automatique (Step B + C + comparaison)

### Ex√©cution faite

Commande lanc√©e avec succ√®s :
- `python tools/rf_run_all_theory.py --out_dir outputs/theory_rf --n_t 70 --n_tau 80 --tau_max 10000 --n_mc 40000`

### Sorties g√©n√©r√©es

Dans `outputs/theory_rf` :

- Arrays :  
  - `step_b_beta_spec_arrays.npz`
  - `step_c_beta_glass_arrays.npz`
  - `step_d_combined_phase_window.npz`
- CSV surfaces :  
  - `step_b_beta_spec_surface.csv`
  - `step_b_lambda_rf_surface.csv`
  - `step_b_lambda_parallel_surface.csv`
  - `step_b_lambda_perp_surface.csv`
  - `step_c_beta_glass_approx_surface.csv`
  - `step_c_beta_glass_exact_surface.csv`
  - `step_c_R_tau_surface.csv`
  - `step_c_v_tau_surface.csv`
  - `step_d_beta_gap_surface.csv`
- Figures :  
  - `step_b_beta_spec_heatmap.png`
  - `step_b_beta_spec_slices.png`
  - `step_b_lambda_rf_heatmap.png`
  - `step_c_beta_glass_approx_heatmap.png`
  - `step_c_beta_glass_exact_heatmap.png`
  - `step_c_R_tau_slices.png`
  - `step_d_phase_window_heatmap.png`
  - `step_d_beta_slices.png`
- R√©sum√©s :  
  - `step_b_beta_spec_summary.json`
  - `step_c_beta_glass_summary.json`

### Point important

- Tu as bien les deux niveaux pour le glass :
  - **approx REM** via \(v(t,\tau)\)
  - **exact GMM** via \(y_\alpha\) (r√©solution num√©rique de \(y-\log y-1=2\alpha\))

Si tu veux, je peux maintenant te faire une **version ‚Äúpaper-ready‚ÄĚ** :
- m√™me calcul mais avec **nomenclature stricte de ton manuscrit**,
- et un script unique qui exporte aussi un **tableau LaTeX** (avec min/max/phase window) pour insertion directe dans `report.tex`.

---

_**User**_

Commande rapide :

python rf_mcl_phase_diagram_experiments.py --quick --outdir ./rf_mcl_results

Commande plus s√©rieuse :

python rf_mcl_phase_diagram_experiments.py \
  --d 64 \
  --p 192 \
  --n-train 12000 \
  --n-eval 5000 \
  --power-iters 35 \
  --n-emp 4000 \
  --outdir ./rf_mcl_results

Le script sauvegarde des CSV et, si matplotlib est disponible, des figures @scripts/rf_mcl_phase_diagram_experiments.py:1-789 

---

### Ex√©cut√©

- **Run rapide**  
  `python rf_mcl_phase_diagram_experiments.py --quick --outdir ./rf_mcl_results`
- **Run s√©rieux**  
  `python rf_mcl_phase_diagram_experiments.py --d 64 --p 192 --n-train 12000 --n-eval 5000 --power-iters 35 --n-emp 4000 --outdir ./rf_mcl_results`

### R√©sultat

Le dossier `scripts/rf_mcl_results` contient bien les CSV + figures :

- `A_population_tau_inf.csv`
- `B_population_finite_tau.csv`
- `C_empirical_REM_glass.csv`

- `B_unlearned_filter_eps_c.png`
- `C_beta_glass_vs_tau.png`
- `C_entropy_collapse_beta_scaled.png`
- `README_run.txt`

### Points observ√©s (run s√©rieux)

- **Step A** : \(\beta_{\text{spec}}\) et \(\beta_{\text{class}}\) calcul√©s pour plusieurs \(t\), avec `align_m` √©lev√© sur une bonne partie du r√©gime.
- **Step B** : en \(\tau\) fini, `eps_c` d√©cro√ģt vers 0 et \(\beta_{\text{spec}}\) √©volue comme attendu avec l‚Äôapprentissage.
- **Step C** : \(\beta_{\text{glass}}\) d√©cro√ģt fortement quand \(\tau\) augmente (coh√©rent avec l‚Äôactivation progressive des modes empiriques).

Si tu veux, je peux maintenant te faire une **lecture courte des courbes** (o√Ļ se trouve la fen√™tre \(\beta_{\text{spec}}<\beta<\beta_{\text{glass}}\) la plus large).

C‚Äôest fait, les deux runs ont bien tourn√© jusqu‚Äôau bout ‚úÖ

### Commandes ex√©cut√©es

- `python rf_mcl_gmm_expert_training_v3.py --d 64 --p 256 --n-train 6000 --n-test 3000 --steps 4000 --calib-steps 1200 --outdir ./gmm_v3`
- `python rf_mcl_gmm_expert_training_v3.py --d 64 --p 256 --steps 4000 --beta-grid --outdir ./gmm_v3_grid`

### R√©sultats g√©n√©r√©s

Dans `scripts/gmm_v3` :
- `SUMMARY.txt`
- `metrics_all.csv` + CSV par variante (`metrics_uniform.csv`, `metrics_hard_cold.csv`, `metrics_fixed_class.csv`, `metrics_fixed_good.csv`, `metrics_theory_anneal.csv`)
- Figures PNG (`metric_beta.png`, `metric_test_best_mse.png`, `metric_test_usage_entropy.png`, etc.)
- Checkpoints `.pt` des experts

Dans `scripts/gmm_v3_grid` :
- `SUMMARY.txt`
- `metrics_all.csv` + CSV des variantes de base + CSV des variantes grille (`metrics_grid_*.csv`)
- Figures PNG mises √† jour avec la grille beta
- Checkpoints `.pt` des variantes grille (`grid_0.0918696_experts.pt`, ‚Ä¶, `grid_87.5192_experts.pt`)

Donc setup ‚Äúcomplet‚ÄĚ avec grille de \(\beta\) : OK.  
Si tu veux, je peux te sortir maintenant un **top-3 des \(\beta\)** selon `test_best_mse` directement depuis `metrics_all.csv`.

---

_**User**_

run les 2 setups python rf_mcl_cifar_experiments.py --quick --outdir ./rf_mcl_cifar_quick

Commande plus s√©rieuse :

python rf_mcl_cifar_experiments.py \
  --data-root ./data \
  --classes automobile horse \
  --d-mode full \
  --p 512 \
  --n-train 8000 \
  --n-eval 2500 \
  --power-iters 30 \
  --outdir ./rf_mcl_cifar

Pour aller plus vite avec PCA :

python rf_mcl_cifar_experiments.py \
  --classes automobile horse \
  --d-mode pca \
  --pca-dim 512 \
  --p 512 \
  --n-train 6000 \
  --n-eval 2000 \
  --outdir ./rf_mcl_cifar_pca 

---

C‚Äôest fait ‚úÖ Les 3 runs ont √©t√© ex√©cut√©s avec succ√®s.

### Commandes lanc√©es (adapt√©es au script existant)
Le script pr√©sent est `rf_mcl_cifar_expert_training_v2.py` (pas `rf_mcl_cifar_experiments.py`), avec `--n-test` au lieu de `--n-eval`.

1) Quick  
`python rf_mcl_cifar_expert_training_v2.py --quick --outdir ./rf_mcl_cifar_quick`

2) S√©rieux full  
`python rf_mcl_cifar_expert_training_v2.py --data-root ./data --classes automobile horse --d-mode full --p 512 --n-train 8000 --n-test 2500 --power-iters 30 --outdir ./rf_mcl_cifar`

3) PCA rapide  
`python rf_mcl_cifar_expert_training_v2.py --classes automobile horse --d-mode pca --pca-dim 512 --p 512 --n-train 6000 --n-test 2000 --outdir ./rf_mcl_cifar_pca`

---

### O√Ļ sont les sorties

- Quick :  
  `outputs/rf_mcl_results/rf_mcl_cifar_quick/`
- Full :  
  `scripts/rf_mcl_cifar/`
- PCA :  
  `scripts/rf_mcl_cifar_pca/`

Chaque dossier contient au moins :
- `SUMMARY.txt`
- `metrics_all.csv`
- `metrics_uniform.csv`
- `metrics_hard_cold.csv`
- `metrics_fixed_class.csv`
- `metrics_fixed_good.csv`
- `metrics_theory_anneal.csv`
- `calibration.json`

Si tu veux, je te fais maintenant un comparatif court **quick vs full vs pca** depuis les `metrics_all.csv` (meilleure variante, gap perf, stabilit√©).

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** ‚ÄĘ Run command: python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path

root = Path('/Data/janis.aiad/DataLab')
out_dir = root / 'outputs' / 'md_csv_bundle'
out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

patterns = [
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/*.csv',
]

all_files = []
for pat in patterns:
    all_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

# keep unique files that exist, deterministic order
uniq = sorted({p.resolve() for p in all_files if p.is_file()})

# keep only files in repo
uniq = [p for p in uniq if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]

index_lines = [
    '# Export CSV en Markdown',
    '',
    'Ce dossier contient un fichier `.md` par CSV (CIFAR + MCL precedent).',
    '',
    f'Total CSV exportes: {len(uniq)}',
    '',
    '## Fichiers',
    '',
]

for i, p in enumerate(uniq, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    safe_name = '__'.join(rel.parts).replace('.csv', '.md')
    target = out_dir / safe_name
    csv_text = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    md = []
    md.append(f'# {rel}')
    md.append('')
    md.append('```csv')
    md.append(csv_text.rstrip('\n'))
    md.append('```')
    md.append('')
    target.write_text('\n'.join(md), encoding='utf-8')
    index_lines.append(f'{i}. `{rel}` -> `{safe_name}`')

(out_dir / 'INDEX.md').write_text('\n'.join(index_lines) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')
print(f'WROTE {len(uniq)} markdown files to {out_dir}')
PY</summary>

```bash
python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path

root = Path('/Data/janis.aiad/DataLab')
out_dir = root / 'outputs' / 'md_csv_bundle'
out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

patterns = [
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/*.csv',
]

all_files = []
for pat in patterns:
    all_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

# keep unique files that exist, deterministic order
uniq = sorted({p.resolve() for p in all_files if p.is_file()})

# keep only files in repo
uniq = [p for p in uniq if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]

index_lines = [
    '# Export CSV en Markdown',
    '',
    'Ce dossier contient un fichier `.md` par CSV (CIFAR + MCL precedent).',
    '',
    f'Total CSV exportes: {len(uniq)}',
    '',
    '## Fichiers',
    '',
]

for i, p in enumerate(uniq, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    safe_name = '__'.join(rel.parts).replace('.csv', '.md')
    target = out_dir / safe_name
    csv_text = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    md = []
    md.append(f'# {rel}')
    md.append('')
    md.append('```csv')
    md.append(csv_text.rstrip('\n'))
    md.append('```')
    md.append('')
    target.write_text('\n'.join(md), encoding='utf-8')
    index_lines.append(f'{i}. `{rel}` -> `{safe_name}`')

(out_dir / 'INDEX.md').write_text('\n'.join(index_lines) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')
print(f'WROTE {len(uniq)} markdown files to {out_dir}')
PY
```

J‚Äôai cr√©√© un dossier Markdown avec **tous les CSV CIFAR + MCL pr√©c√©dent** :

- `outputs/md_csv_bundle/`

Contenu :
- `INDEX.md` (table de correspondance)
- **43 fichiers `.md`**, chacun contenant un CSV en bloc :

```csv
...
```

Tu peux donc ouvrir n‚Äôimporte quel `.md` et copier-coller directement.

Si tu veux, je peux aussi te g√©n√©rer une **version unique** `ALL_CSV_IN_ONE.md` (un seul gros fichier).

---

_**User**_

mais non mais dedans je veux les contenus exact ptdr avec toutes les metriques .md et tous les csv !

---

_**Agent (default)**_

Compris. Je refais √ßa en **full exact** : je te g√©n√®re un dossier avec  
1) **tous les CSV copi√©s tels quels**,  
2) un **gros `.md` unique** qui contient le **contenu brut complet** de chaque CSV (sans r√©sum√©),  
3) les `SUMMARY.txt` complets aussi dans un `.md`.

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** ‚ÄĘ Run command: python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

root = Path('/Data/janis.aiad/DataLab')
out = root / 'outputs' / 'md_csv_bundle_full_exact'
out.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# Collect CSV + summary files from relevant experiment areas
patterns_csv = [
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/*.csv',
]
patterns_summary = [
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/SUMMARY.txt',
]

csv_files = []
for pat in patterns_csv:
    csv_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

summary_files = []
for pat in patterns_summary:
    summary_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

csv_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in csv_files if p.is_file()})
summary_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in summary_files if p.is_file()})

csv_files = [p for p in csv_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]
summary_files = [p for p in summary_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]

# 1) Copy all CSV files exact into folder (flattened name with path encoded)
csv_copy_dir = out / 'csv_exact_copies'
csv_copy_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
for p in csv_files:
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    name = '__'.join(rel.parts)
    shutil.copy2(p, csv_copy_dir / name)

# 2) Big markdown with raw exact content of every CSV
all_csv_md = []
all_csv_md.append('# ALL CSV RAW (exact content)')
all_csv_md.append('')
all_csv_md.append(f'Total CSV: {len(csv_files)}')
all_csv_md.append('')
for i, p in enumerate(csv_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    all_csv_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    all_csv_md.append('')
    all_csv_md.append('```csv')
    all_csv_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    all_csv_md.append('```')
    all_csv_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(all_csv_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# 3) Big markdown with all SUMMARY files exact content
all_summary_md = []
all_summary_md.append('# ALL SUMMARY RAW (exact content)')
all_summary_md.append('')
all_summary_md.append(f'Total SUMMARY files: {len(summary_files)}')
all_summary_md.append('')
for i, p in enumerate(summary_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    all_summary_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    all_summary_md.append('')
    all_summary_md.append('```text')
    all_summary_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    all_summary_md.append('```')
    all_summary_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(all_summary_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# 4) Index
idx = []
idx.append('# INDEX FULL EXACT')
idx.append('')
idx.append(f'- CSV total: {len(csv_files)}')
idx.append(f'- SUMMARY total: {len(summary_files)}')
idx.append('')
idx.append('## Main files')
idx.append('')
idx.append('- `ALL_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md`')

```bash
python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

root = Path('/Data/janis.aiad/DataLab')
out = root / 'outputs' / 'md_csv_bundle_full_exact'
out.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# Collect CSV + summary files from relevant experiment areas
patterns_csv = [
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/*.csv',
]
patterns_summary = [
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/SUMMARY.txt',
]

csv_files = []
for pat in patterns_csv:
    csv_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

summary_files = []
for pat in patterns_summary:
    summary_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

csv_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in csv_files if p.is_file()})
summary_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in summary_files if p.is_file()})

csv_files = [p for p in csv_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]
summary_files = [p for p in summary_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]

# 1) Copy all CSV files exact into folder (flattened name with path encoded)
csv_copy_dir = out / 'csv_exact_copies'
csv_copy_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
for p in csv_files:
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    name = '__'.join(rel.parts)
    shutil.copy2(p, csv_copy_dir / name)

# 2) Big markdown with raw exact content of every CSV
all_csv_md = []
all_csv_md.append('# ALL CSV RAW (exact content)')
all_csv_md.append('')
all_csv_md.append(f'Total CSV: {len(csv_files)}')
all_csv_md.append('')
for i, p in enumerate(csv_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    all_csv_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    all_csv_md.append('')
    all_csv_md.append('```csv')
    all_csv_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    all_csv_md.append('```')
    all_csv_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(all_csv_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# 3) Big markdown with all SUMMARY files exact content
all_summary_md = []
all_summary_md.append('# ALL SUMMARY RAW (exact content)')
all_summary_md.append('')
all_summary_md.append(f'Total SUMMARY files: {len(summary_files)}')
all_summary_md.append('')
for i, p in enumerate(summary_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    all_summary_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    all_summary_md.append('')
    all_summary_md.append('```text')
    all_summary_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    all_summary_md.append('```')
    all_summary_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(all_summary_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# 4) Index
idx = []
idx.append('# INDEX FULL EXACT')
idx.append('')
idx.append(f'- CSV total: {len(csv_files)}')
idx.append(f'- SUMMARY total: {len(summary_files)}')
idx.append('')
idx.append('## Main files')
idx.append('')
idx.append('- `ALL_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md`')
idx.append('- `ALL_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md`')
idx.append('- `csv_exact_copies/` (copie exacte de tous les CSV)')
idx.append('')
idx.append('## CSV list')
idx.append('')
for p in csv_files:
    idx.append(f'- `{p.relative_to(root)}`')
idx.append('')
idx.append('## SUMMARY list')
idx.append('')
for p in summary_files:
    idx.append(f'- `{p.relative_to(root)}`')
(out / 'INDEX.md').write_text('\n'.join(idx) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

print(f'WROTE FULL EXACT BUNDLE -> {out}')
print(f'CSV: {len(csv_files)} | SUMMARY: {len(summary_files)}')
PY

<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** ‚ÄĘ Run command: python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

root = Path('/Data/janis.aiad/DataLab')
out = root / 'outputs' / 'md_results_bundle_latest'
out.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

patterns_csv = [
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v4_router/**/*.csv',
    '**/cifar_v3_router_pca/**/*.csv',
]
patterns_summary = [
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/gmm_v4_router/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/cifar_v3_router_pca/**/SUMMARY.txt',
]

csv_files = []
for pat in patterns_csv:
    csv_files.extend(root.glob(pat))
summary_files = []
for pat in patterns_summary:
    summary_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

csv_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in csv_files if p.is_file()})
summary_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in summary_files if p.is_file()})
csv_files = [p for p in csv_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]
summary_files = [p for p in summary_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]

# Copy exact files
csv_copy_dir = out / 'csv_exact_copies'
csv_copy_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
for p in csv_files:
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    name = '__'.join(rel.parts)
    shutil.copy2(p, csv_copy_dir / name)

# Big CSV markdown
csv_md = ['# ALL RESULTS CSV (RAW EXACT)', '', f'Total CSV files: {len(csv_files)}', '']
for i, p in enumerate(csv_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    csv_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    csv_md.append('')
    csv_md.append('```csv')
    csv_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    csv_md.append('```')
    csv_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_RESULTS_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(csv_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# Big summaries markdown
sum_md = ['# ALL RESULTS SUMMARY (RAW EXACT)', '', f'Total SUMMARY files: {len(summary_files)}', '']
for i, p in enumerate(summary_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    sum_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    sum_md.append('')
    sum_md.append('```text')
    sum_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    sum_md.append('```')
    sum_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_RESULTS_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(sum_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# Index
idx = [
    '# RESULTS BUNDLE INDEX',
    '',
    f'- CSV files: {len(csv_files)}',
    f'- SUMMARY files: {len(summary_files)}',
    '',
    '## Main outputs',
    '',
    '- `ALL_RESULTS_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md`',
    '- `ALL_RESULTS_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md`',
    '- `csv_exact_copies/`',
    '',
    '## Included CSV files',
    '',
]
idx.extend([f'- `{p.relative_to(root)}`' for p in csv_files])
idx.extend(['', '## Included SUMMARY files', ''])
idx.extend([f'- `{p.relative_to(root)}`' for p in summary_files])
(out / 'INDEX.md').write_text('\n'.join(idx) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

print(f'WROTE bundle at: {out}')
print(f'CSV={len(csv_files)} SUMMARY={len(summary_files)}')
PY</summary>

```bash
python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
import shutil

root = Path('/Data/janis.aiad/DataLab')
out = root / 'outputs' / 'md_results_bundle_latest'
out.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

patterns_csv = [
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/*.csv',
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/*.csv',
    '**/gmm_v4_router/**/*.csv',
    '**/cifar_v3_router_pca/**/*.csv',
]
patterns_summary = [
    '**/rf_mcl_results/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_corrected/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/rf_mcl_cifar*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/gmm_v3*/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/gmm_v4_router/**/SUMMARY.txt',
    '**/cifar_v3_router_pca/**/SUMMARY.txt',
]

csv_files = []
for pat in patterns_csv:
    csv_files.extend(root.glob(pat))
summary_files = []
for pat in patterns_summary:
    summary_files.extend(root.glob(pat))

csv_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in csv_files if p.is_file()})
summary_files = sorted({p.resolve() for p in summary_files if p.is_file()})
csv_files = [p for p in csv_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]
summary_files = [p for p in summary_files if str(p).startswith(str(root.resolve()))]

# Copy exact files
csv_copy_dir = out / 'csv_exact_copies'
csv_copy_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
for p in csv_files:
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    name = '__'.join(rel.parts)
    shutil.copy2(p, csv_copy_dir / name)

# Big CSV markdown
csv_md = ['# ALL RESULTS CSV (RAW EXACT)', '', f'Total CSV files: {len(csv_files)}', '']
for i, p in enumerate(csv_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    csv_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    csv_md.append('')
    csv_md.append('```csv')
    csv_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    csv_md.append('```')
    csv_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_RESULTS_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(csv_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# Big summaries markdown
sum_md = ['# ALL RESULTS SUMMARY (RAW EXACT)', '', f'Total SUMMARY files: {len(summary_files)}', '']
for i, p in enumerate(summary_files, 1):
    rel = p.relative_to(root)
    txt = p.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')
    sum_md.append(f'## {i}. `{rel}`')
    sum_md.append('')
    sum_md.append('```text')
    sum_md.append(txt.rstrip('\n'))
    sum_md.append('```')
    sum_md.append('')
(out / 'ALL_RESULTS_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md').write_text('\n'.join(sum_md) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

# Index
idx = [
    '# RESULTS BUNDLE INDEX',
    '',
    f'- CSV files: {len(csv_files)}',
    f'- SUMMARY files: {len(summary_files)}',
    '',
    '## Main outputs',

    '- `ALL_RESULTS_CSV_RAW_EXACT.md`',
    '- `ALL_RESULTS_SUMMARY_RAW_EXACT.md`',
    '- `csv_exact_copies/`',
    '',
    '## Included CSV files',
    '',
]
idx.extend([f'- `{p.relative_to(root)}`' for p in csv_files])
idx.extend(['', '## Included SUMMARY files', ''])
idx.extend([f'- `{p.relative_to(root)}`' for p in summary_files])
(out / 'INDEX.md').write_text('\n'.join(idx) + '\n', encoding='utf-8')

print(f'WROTE bundle at: {out}')
print(f'CSV={len(csv_files)} SUMMARY={len(summary_files)}')

Verdict : la v4 router n‚Äôa pas vraiment test√© le ph√©nom√®ne voulu. Elle r√©v√®le surtout un probl√®me de target/√©chelle et confirme que, dans les runs actuels, les responsabilit√©s MCL sont quasi uniformes, donc le routeur n‚Äôa rien √† apprendre.

Donc : les r√©sultats GMM v4 ne sont pas comparables aux r√©sultats v3. La v4 a chang√© la t√Ęche.

Le patch minimal est :

def sample_gmm(n, means, sigma0, t, device):
    C, d = means.shape
    labels = torch.randint(0, C, (n,), device=device)
    x0 = means[labels] + sigma0 * torch.randn(n, d, device=device)

    gamma = math.exp(-t)
    eps = torch.randn_like(x0)
    xt = gamma * x0 + math.sqrt(max(1.0 - gamma * gamma, 1e-12)) * eps

    return xt, eps, labels   # pas x0

M√™me chose c√īt√© CIFAR : il faut garder le bruit utilis√© dans la diffusion.

def diffuse_with_target(x0, t):
    gamma = math.exp(-t)
    eps = torch.randn_like(x0)
    xt = gamma * x0 + math.sqrt(max(1.0 - gamma * gamma, 1e-12)) * eps
    return xt, eps

Puis :

Xtr_t, Ytr = diffuse_with_target(Xtr0, params.t)
Xte_t, Yte = diffuse_with_target(Xte0, params.t)

y_train = Ytr.to(device)
y_test = Yte.to(device)
2. La calibration v4 est aussi cass√©e par ce changement d‚Äô√©chelle

En GMM v3 :

En GMM v4 :

Donc la v4 utilise des ő≤ environ 20 fois trop petits pour produire des responsabilit√©s non uniformes. √Ä ő≤=0.05, les gaps de co√Ľts sont invisibles pour le softmax, donc q

router_vs_teacher_ce = 1.386294...
router_vs_teacher_kl ‚Čą 0

Le routeur n‚Äô√©choue pas : le teacher est vide.

3. Ce que disent quand m√™me les anciens r√©sultats

Les runs v3/v2 restent tr√®s informatifs.

Sur GMM v3, theory_anneal est le meilleur en MSE :

theory_anneal test_best_mse = 0.0338
uniform       test_best_mse = 0.0379

et l‚Äôalignement devient √©norme :

test_expert_align_m = 0.960

Mais la sp√©cialisation classe est nulle :

test_class_mi_norm = 0.00028

Donc l‚Äôannealing apprend quelque chose de r√©el, mais pas une partition s√©mantique par classe.

Sur CIFAR v2 PCA, m√™me conclusion : theory_anneal descend jusqu‚Äô√† 0.320, meilleur que uniform √† 0.342, mais avec

test_class_mi_norm = 0.0
test_eff_frac_min ‚Čą 0.00097

Donc c‚Äôest une am√©lioration oracle/glassy/collapse, pas une sp√©cialisation classe exploitable.

4. Le test Bayes-router GMM est tr√®s parlant

En v4 GMM, le bayes_class_mi_norm vaut 0.23, donc x
t
	‚Äč

 contient bien de l‚Äôinformation de classe. Pourtant :

bayes_router_mix_mse ‚Čą router_mix_mse ‚Čą soft_oracle_mse

Donc le probl√®me n‚Äôest pas seulement que le routeur ne voit pas la classe. Le probl√®me est que les experts appris ne sont pas align√©s avec les classes. M√™me un routeur bay√©sien exact p(c‚ą£x
t
	‚Äč

) ne gagne rien.

5. La vraie v5 √† faire

Il faut faire une v5 avec quatre corrections :

Target diffusion bruit/score, pas x
0
	‚Äč

, pour √™tre comparable √† v3.
Reporter explicitement l‚Äôentropie moyenne des responsabilit√©s :
teacher_entropy_norm = H(q_star).mean() / log(K)

Si teacher_entropy_norm > 0.95, le routeur est inutile. Si < 0.05, on est en collapse dur.

R√©utiliser la calibration v3/corrected, pas la calibration v4. Pour GMM √† t‚Čą2.05, il faut balayer autour de :
beta ‚Čą 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1.1

pas autour de 0.02.

Conclusion nette : les r√©sultats actuels ne montrent pas une sp√©cialisation classe d√©ployable. Ils montrent que le canal th√©orique existe, mais que le MCL brut choisit soit le mode commun, soit un collapse/glass, sauf si on force une g√©om√©trie r√©siduelle ou √©quilibr√©e

_**Agent (default)**_

Parfait, c‚Äôest fait.

J‚Äôai cr√©√© une version MNIST adapt√©e de la derni√®re logique CIFAR, avec les points que tu voulais :

- **nouveau script** : `scripts/rf_mcl_mnist_v1_router.py`
- **target diffusion corrig√©e** : le mod√®le pr√©dit bien le **bruit \(\epsilon\)** (pas \(x_0\))
- ajout du diagnostic :
  - `teacher_entropy_norm` (normalis√© par \(\log K\))
- pipeline complet comme CIFAR :
  - calibration
  - variantes MCL (`uniform`, `fixed_class`, `fixed_good`, `hard_cold`, `theory_anneal`)
  - entra√ģnement routeur
  - export CSV/JSON/SUMMARY/checkpoints/figures

## Test ex√©cut√©

Commande lanc√©e avec succ√®s :

```bash
python rf_mcl_mnist_v1_router.py --quick --device cuda --outdir ./mnist_v1_router_quick
```

Sortie principale : `scripts/mnist_v1_router_quick/`

Le run confirme ton diagnostic :
- pour la plupart des variantes (hors hard-cold), on a encore
  - `teacher_entropy_norm ‚Čą 1.0`
  - `router_vs_teacher_ce ‚Čą log(4)`
  - `router_vs_teacher_kl ‚Čą 0`
- donc teacher quasi uniforme \(\Rightarrow\) routeur peu informatif.
- `hard_cold` casse cette uniformit√© (entropie teacher plus basse), mais au prix d‚Äôun comportement plus dur/collapse-like.

Si tu veux, je fais directement une **v2 MNIST** avec tes contraintes fortes :
1) calibration \(\beta\) recentr√©e (range class-driven),  
2) residualization \(f_k = f_0 + g_k\),  
3) seuil automatique ‚Äúteacher useless‚ÄĚ si `teacher_entropy_norm > 0.95`,  
et je te lance un run complet derri√®re.

Donc : les r√©sultats GMM v4 ne sont pas comparables aux r√©sultats v3. La v4 a chang√© la t√Ęche.

Le patch minimal est :

def sample_gmm(n, means, sigma0, t, device):
    C, d = means.shape
    labels = torch.randint(0, C, (n,), device=device)
    x0 = means[labels] + sigma0 * torch.randn(n, d, device=device)

    gamma = math.exp(-t)
    eps = torch.randn_like(x0)
    xt = gamma * x0 + math.sqrt(max(1.0 - gamma * gamma, 1e-12)) * eps

    return xt, eps, labels   # pas x0

M√™me chose c√īt√© CIFAR : il faut garder le bruit utilis√© dans la diffusion.

def diffuse_with_target(x0, t):
    gamma = math.exp(-t)
    eps = torch.randn_like(x0)
    xt = gamma * x0 + math.sqrt(max(1.0 - gamma * gamma, 1e-12)) * eps
    return xt, eps

Puis :

Xtr_t, Ytr = diffuse_with_target(Xtr0, params.t)
Xte_t, Yte = diffuse_with_target(Xte0, params.t)

y_train = Ytr.to(device)
y_test = Yte.to(device)
2. La calibration v4 est aussi cass√©e par ce changement d‚Äô√©chelle

En GMM v3 :

beta_trans = 3.15
beta_target = 1.10

En GMM v4 :

beta_class = 0.017
beta_trans = 0.714

Donc la v4 utilise des ő≤ environ 20 fois trop petits pour produire des responsabilit√©s non uniformes. √Ä ő≤=0.05, les gaps de co√Ľts sont invisibles pour le softmax, donc q
‚čÜ
 reste uniforme. √áa explique directement :

router_vs_teacher_kl ‚Čą 0

Le routeur n‚Äô√©choue pas : le teacher est vide.

3. Ce que disent quand m√™me les anciens r√©sultats

Les runs v3/v2 restent tr√®s informatifs.

Sur GMM v3, theory_anneal est le meilleur en MSE :

theory_anneal test_best_mse = 0.0338
uniform       test_best_mse = 0.0379

et l‚Äôalignement devient √©norme :

test_expert_align_m = 0.960

Mais la sp√©cialisation classe est nulle :

test_class_mi_norm = 0.00028

Donc l‚Äôannealing apprend quelque chose de r√©el, mais pas une partition s√©mantique par classe.

Sur CIFAR v2 PCA, m√™me conclusion : theory_anneal descend jusqu‚Äô√† 0.320, meilleur que uniform √† 0.342, mais avec

test_class_mi_norm = 0.0
test_eff_frac_min ‚Čą 0.00097

Donc c‚Äôest une am√©lioration oracle/glassy/collapse, pas une sp√©cialisation classe exploitable.

4. Le test Bayes-router GMM est tr√®s parlant

En v4 GMM, le bayes_class_mi_norm vaut 0.23, donc x
t
	‚Äč

 contient bien de l‚Äôinformation de classe. Pourtant :

bayes_router_mix_mse ‚Čą router_mix_mse ‚Čą soft_oracle_mse

Donc le probl√®me n‚Äôest pas seulement que le routeur ne voit pas la classe. Le probl√®me est que les experts appris ne sont pas align√©s avec les classes. M√™me un routeur bay√©sien exact p(c‚ą£x
t
	‚Äč

) ne gagne rien.

5. La vraie v5 √† faire

Il faut faire une v5 avec quatre corrections :

Target diffusion bruit/score, pas x
0
	‚Äč

, pour √™tre comparable √† v3.
Reporter explicitement l‚Äôentropie moyenne des responsabilit√©s :
teacher_entropy_norm = H(q_star).mean() / log(K)

Si teacher_entropy_norm > 0.95, le routeur est inutile. Si < 0.05, on est en collapse dur.

R√©utiliser la calibration v3/corrected, pas la calibration v4. Pour GMM √† t‚Čą2.05, il faut balayer autour de :
beta ‚Čą 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1.1

pas autour de 0.02.

Tester la version residualis√©e :

Conclusion nette : les r√©sultats actuels ne montrent pas une sp√©cialisation classe d√©ployable. Ils montrent que le canal th√©orique existe, mais que le MCL brut choisit soit le mode commun, soit un collapse/glass, sauf si on force une g√©om√©trie r√©siduelle ou √©quilibr√©e

Oui, et ton diagnostic est **correct** ‚ÄĒ et je l‚Äôai v√©rifi√© empiriquement.

Ce que j‚Äôai pris en compte :
- **MNIST v1** : j‚Äôai bien appliqu√© la correction target diffusion (pr√©dire \(\epsilon\), pas \(x_0\)) + `teacher_entropy_norm`.
- Sur les runs r√©cents CIFAR/GMM, on retrouve exactement ton signal :
  - `router_vs_teacher_ce ‚Čą log(K)`
  - `router_vs_teacher_kl ‚Čą 0`
  - `router_mix_mse ‚Čą soft_oracle_mse ‚Čą mean_expert_mse`
  -> teacher quasi uniforme, routeur quasi inutile.

Je viens m√™me de lancer un run CIFAR ‚Äúscale‚ÄĚ (10 classes, \(K=8\), beta-grid) et √ßa confirme :
- `router_vs_teacher_ce ‚Čą 2.07944 = log(8)`
- `router_vs_teacher_kl ~ 1e-8`
- pas de sp√©cialisation exploitable.

Donc oui : **tes remarques sont valid√©es**.  
En revanche, je n‚Äôai pas encore patch√© **CIFAR v3** et **GMM v4** avec la version compl√®te v5 (target \(\epsilon\) + calibration recentr√©e + residualisation \(f_k=f_0+g_k\)).

Si tu veux, je le fais maintenant directement :
1) `rf_mcl_gmm_v5_router.py`  
2) `rf_mcl_cifar_v5_router.py`  
avec tes 4 corrections, puis je lance quick + run s√©rieux et je te livre les CSV/SUMMARY.

C‚Äôest fait: j‚Äôai patch√© et relanc√© en **v5** avec tes 4 corrections sur GMM et CIFAR.

### Ce que j‚Äôai patch√©

- Nouveau script `scripts/rf_mcl_gmm_v5_router.py`
- Nouveau script `scripts/rf_mcl_cifar_v5_router.py`
- Corrections int√©gr√©es:
  - target diffusion = **\(\epsilon\)** (plus `x0`)
  - diagnostic **`teacher_entropy_norm`** report√© partout
  - calibration recentr√©e avec grille beta incluant `0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 1.1`
  - **residualisation** experts: \(f_k = f_0 + g_k\) (avec t√™te ridge pour \(f_0\), experts sur r√©sidu)

### Runs ex√©cut√©s

- **Quick**
  - `outputs/rf_mcl_results/gmm_v5_quick/`
  - `outputs/rf_mcl_results/cifar_v5_quick/`
- **S√©rieux**
  - `outputs/rf_mcl_results/gmm_v5_serious/`
  - `outputs/rf_mcl_results/cifar_v5_serious/`

### Livrables g√©n√©r√©s

Dans chaque dossier:
- `metrics_all.csv`
- `router_final_metrics.csv`
- `SUMMARY.txt`

Et pour CIFAR aussi:
- `calibration.json`
- `data_info.json`
- `params.json`

### Observation cl√© (coh√©rente avec ton verdict)

Sur quick + s√©rieux, GMM et CIFAR:
- `teacher_entropy_norm` reste ~`1.0`
- `router_vs_teacher_ce` ~ `log(K)` (ici ~`1.386` pour `K=4`)
- donc teacher quasi uniforme, router peu informatif, m√™me apr√®s patch v5 actuel.

Si tu veux, prochaine √©tape: je te fais maintenant un **bundle markdown brut exact** (CSV + SUMMARY complets, copiable) pour ces 4 nouveaux runs, comme ton format pr√©c√©dent.
